issues_found: Cross-validation fold leakage in a temporal setting: The inner loop of the nested cross-validation uses '5 stratified folds' on the training set, but stratified k-fold does not respect temporal ordering. Patients from later time periods will appear in training folds while earlier patients appear in validation folds, allowing the model to implicitly learn from future data during tuning. This should be time-series cross-validation with forward-chaining folds instead.

Youden threshold selected on validation set but reported alongside test metrics: The design states sensitivity and specificity are computed at the 'Youden-optimal threshold on the validation set.' If this threshold selection informed any decisions prior to test-set lock, it is a mild form of evaluation contamination. More importantly, reporting validation-derived thresholds alongside test-set metrics without confidence intervals conflates two different evaluation regimes and may overstate operational performance.

Stratification on 'baseline sepsis risk quartile' computed from full cohort before splitting: The design mentions stratifying splits by 'baseline sepsis risk quartile derived from age, admission type, and Elixhauser score.' If these quartile boundaries are derived from the full dataset before the chronological split is applied, this is a subtle leakage: test-set patients' characteristics influenced the stratification buckets used during training and validation. Quartile boundaries should be derived from the training portion only.

Pre-trained transformer embeddings are frozen and not fine-tuned, yet the embedding quality is treated as equivalent across settings: Using a static pre-trained clinical BERT without any fine-tuning on the in-domain task means the embeddings may not be optimally sensitive to sepsis-relevant linguistic signals. The design does not ablate whether fine-tuning the transformer on the training split (with careful temporal containment) would change conclusions. This is not necessarily a fatal flaw, but it means the experiment may underestimate the ceiling contribution of free text, making a null result ambiguous.

Attention pooling mechanism is trained on what data: The design uses 'attention pooling' to aggregate token embeddings into a fixed-length vector. If the attention pooling weights are learned (i.e., a trainable layer), they must be trained only on training-split data. The design does not specify this clearly. If the attention pooling is fit on all notes including validation or test patients, this is label leakage through the aggregation mechanism.

The 'note count' control feature is included in both models but is asymmetrically informative: Adding note count to both models is described as a confound control, but its inclusion in the structured-only baseline gives that model partial access to documentation-derived signal. If note count is predictive, the structured baseline becomes a partial text-signal model, narrowing the contrast being tested. The cleaner design would be to report a version of the baseline without note count, or to treat it as a separate ablation condition, so the hypothesis about free-text embeddings is isolated more precisely.

No statistical significance testing or confidence intervals on the primary comparison: The hypothesis is framed as 'confirmed if the hybrid model achieves higher test-set AUROC.' A single-point AUROC comparison without a significance test (e.g., DeLong test for correlated AUROCs, bootstrap confidence intervals) or effect-size threshold means a trivially small, clinically meaningless difference could confirm the hypothesis. Given the imbalanced class distribution and tens of thousands of admissions, even very small AUROC differences may appear, and the design provides no principled threshold for what constitutes a meaningful improvement.

Exclusion of admissions with sepsis onset in the 6–72 hour window creates an artificial negative class: Patients who develop sepsis between 6 and 72 hours after the prediction window are excluded. These are likely the most ambiguous, highest-acuity patients and may be systematically different from true negatives. Excluding them sharpens the discrimination task artificially and may inflate AUROC for both models while obscuring how either model handles the clinically important borderline population. The design should at minimum report sensitivity analyses that include these patients with a relabeled or censored outcome.

Multi-instance temporal prediction structure is not clearly specified: For a given patient admission, it is unclear whether a single prediction window is defined (e.g., at a fixed time into admission) or whether rolling predictions are generated across the admission timeline. If multiple prediction windows per patient are evaluated, standard train/val/test splits at the admission level may still allow within-admission temporal leakage (later windows inform model training while earlier windows from the same admission are in a different fold). The design should explicitly state that all windows from a single admission are confined to one split.

Hypothesis alignment with evaluation: The stated hypothesis concerns outperformance under realistic conditions, but the experiment does not include a prospective or shadow-deployment evaluation component, nor does it benchmark against existing clinical scores (e.g., qSOFA, SOFA, NEWS). Without a clinical benchmark, the hypothesis is confirmed in a vacuum — a hybrid model beating an internal structured baseline does not establish clinical utility or readiness, and the design should acknowledge this scope gap more explicitly as a limitation affecting interpretation, not just causal scope.
smoke_response.reasoning: The design is thoughtful and substantially better than typical industry proposals — temporal splitting, preprocessing containment, and test-set lock discipline are all present — but it has several methodological issues serious enough to undermine its conclusions: stratified k-fold in the inner CV loop violates temporal integrity, the attention pooling training data is unspecified and potentially leaky, the primary hypothesis confirmation criterion lacks any statistical significance or minimum effect-size threshold, and the exclusion of the 6–72 hour sepsis group artificially simplifies the negative class in ways that likely inflate discrimination metrics for both models. These issues should be resolved before the experiment is run.
